C:
I have the optimized version ready, but actually running the benchmark is gated behind a permission prompt in this environment (the Write and `node -e` calls each came back needing approval, and I don't want to spam prompts). So here's the faster function plus the speedup worked out analytically — I can run the live benchmark if you approve the command.

## The optimized function

```js
function isPrime(n){
  if (n < 2) return false;
  if (n < 4) return true;             // 2 and 3 are prime
  if (n % 2 === 0) return false;      // any other even number isn't
  for (let i = 3; i * i <= n; i += 2) // test only odd divisors up to √n
    if (n % i === 0) return false;
  return true;
}
```

Two changes do the work:

1. **Stop at √n instead of n.** If `n` has a divisor larger than its square root, it must also have one smaller than it, so you never need to test past `√n`. That alone turns an O(n) loop into O(√n). I used `i * i <= n` rather than `i <= Math.sqrt(n)` to avoid a sqrt call and floating-point rounding.
2. **Skip even divisors.** After ruling out 2, no even number can be a factor, so the loop steps by 2 and tests roughly half as many candidates.

It returns identical results to your original on every input, including the edge cases the original handled via `return n > 1` (0, 1, and negatives all return `false`; 2 and 3 return `true`).

## How much faster

The speedup factor is approximately **2·√n**, and it grows as the numbers get bigger:

| n | old iterations (~n) | new iterations (~√n/2) | ~speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1,000,003 | ~1.0M | ~500 | ~2,000× |
| 10,000,019 | ~10M | ~1,580 | ~6,300× |
| 49,979,687 | ~50M | ~3,535 | ~14,000× |

For a worst case — a large prime, where the old loop runs all the way to `n` — the optimized version does on the order of **thousands of times less work**. For small inputs the gain is modest (for `n` under ~20 they're comparable), and for even numbers the new version returns almost instantly regardless of size.

These are iteration-count estimates, not a wall-clock measurement. Want me to run the actual benchmark? It needs you to approve a `node` command (it'll verify both functions agree on 0–200,000, then time them on the large primes above and print the real speedup).
